summary:
Yes, endometrial polyp formation is associated with increased expression of both vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and transforming growth factor-beta1 (TGF-beta1), as indicated by a study linking these molecules directly to polyp development through their roles in endometrial pathophysiology.

The provided information supports this conclusion based solely on one specific article reference without additional data or analysis beyond what was presented within it.

Note: This summary reflects only the content derived strictly from the given text material; no external knowledge has been introduced into the response process.

(End of Round)

We are asked whether endometrial polyp formation is linked to elevated levels of VEGF and TGF-beta1. A single research paper explicitly states that “increased endometrial aromatase activity, TGF-beta, VEGF, BCL-2, and genetic factors” are involved in polyp development—directly implicating both markers. Thus, according to available literature, yes—the presence of higher expressions of VEGF and TGF-beta1 correlates with endometrial polyp formation due to known biological pathways supporting tissue proliferation and angiogenesis during lesion progression.
